Meanwhile, Carlos unclaims Unit 9 and claims Unit 15 instead — no time wasted, no hunting for Mike to ask what to do.\n\n**Resolution:** Mike sees the block, finds out the plumber called in sick, and reassigns plumbing coverage. By afternoon, Unit 9 is unblocked and another electrician claims it. The blocked-work incident that would have cost an hour of idle time cost Carlos three minutes.\n\n**Capabilities Revealed:** Status change (to Blocked), voice-input notes, unclaim/reclaim flow, block visibility for superintendent, real-time notification of status changes.\n\n### Journey 3: Mike Sets Up the Board (Admin/Setup Path)\n\n**Persona:** Mike, site superintendent. First on site, last to leave. Keeps the entire project in his head — until today.\n\n**Opening Scene:** It's Sunday evening before the Monday kickoff of a new 25-unit remodel. Mike is at his kitchen table with the unit list from the project manager. He's been dreading the usual Monday morning chaos — 15 workers showing up, all asking where to go at once.\n\n**Rising Action:** Mike opens TaskFlow on his laptop. He creates a new project: \"Riverside Apartments.\" He adds 25 units — typing each unit number. For each unit, he tags which trades are needed: demolition first, then plumbing and electrical rough-in, then drywall, then paint. It takes him about 10 minutes. He marks Units 1–5 as \"Available\" for demolition since those are ready to start. The rest stay in a \"Not Ready\" state.\n\n**Climax:** Mike looks at the board and for the first time sees his entire mental model laid out visually. Twenty-five units, five trades, clear statuses. He copies the board link and drops it in the crew group text: \"New app for tomorrow. Open this link when you get to site. Pick your trade, pick a unit, get to work. No more waiting for me.\"\n\n**Resolution:** Monday morning, Mike stands by the trailer and watches. Workers trickle in, pull out phones, and start claiming units. By 7:10am, all five demolition units are claimed and workers are walking to their spots. Nobody asked Mike where to go. He sips his coffee and walks the site checking quality instead of directing traffic.\n\n**Capabilities Revealed:** Project creation, unit management (add/edit), trade-to-unit assignment, bulk status setting, shareable board link (no auth), board overview/dashboard view.\n\n### Journey 4: Mike Manages the Day (Superintendent Monitoring)\n\n**Persona:** Same Mike, two weeks into using TaskFlow. The board is his command center.\n\n**Opening Scene:** It's 9:30am. Mike is walking the site doing quality checks — something he never had time for before TaskFlow. His phone buzzes: Unit 9 just went to \"Blocked\" status.\n\n**Rising Action:** Mike opens the board and sees Carlos's voice note: \"Plumbing not done, can't start electrical.\" He checks the plumbing row — Hector was supposed to finish Unit 9 yesterday but it still shows \"In-Progress.\" Mike calls Hector, who says he got pulled to Unit 14 for an emergency fix. Mike scans the board for available plumbers — Danny just finished Unit 6 and hasn't claimed anything new.\n\n**Climax:** Mike walks over to Danny: \"Unit 9 needs plumbing finished before electrical can start. Can you knock it out this morning?\" Danny claims Unit 9 on the board. Within minutes, the block is being resolved. Mike didn't discover this problem by stumbling into Unit 9 at 2pm — he caught it at 9:30am because the board told him.\n\n**Resolution:** By end of day, Mike reviews the board: 18 of 25 units have active work, 3 are blocked (all with notes explaining why), and 4 are complete. He screenshots the board and texts it to Sarah, the PM. \"Day 9 status — no call needed.\" He saved two hours today that he would have spent answering \"where should I go?\" questions.\n\n**Capabilities Revealed:** Real-time block notifications, voice note visibility, board-wide status overview, worker activity tracking, screenshot-friendly board layout.\n\n### Journey 5: Sarah Checks Progress Remotely (Future — V2)\n\n**Persona:** Sarah, remote project manager. Oversees three remodeling projects from the office.\n\n**Opening Scene:** It's 2pm. The property owner just called Sarah asking for a progress update on Riverside Apartments. Normally she'd have to call Mike, interrupt his work, and get a verbal summary she'd scribble into a spreadsheet.\n\n**Resolution (V2 Vision):** Sarah opens TaskFlow on her laptop, selects the Riverside project, and sees the real-time board. 20 of 25 units show progress. She generates a status snapshot — a simple summary showing units complete, in-progress, and blocked — and emails it to the property owner. Total time: 90 seconds. Mike's phone never rang.\n\n**Capabilities Revealed (V2):** Remote dashboard access, multi-project view, status snapshot/report generation, stakeholder-friendly output.\n\n### Journey Requirements Summary\n\n| Journey | Key Capabilities Revealed |\n|---------|-------------------------|\n| **Carlos Happy Path** | Trade selection, board filtering, one-tap claiming, real-time updates, preference memory |\n| **Carlos Edge Case** | Status changes (Blocked), voice notes, unclaim/reclaim, block notifications |\n| **Mike Setup** | Project creation, unit management, trade-to-unit assignment, shareable link, no-auth access |\n| **Mike Monitoring** | Real-time alerts, board overview, worker activity visibility, screenshot-friendly layout |\n| **Sarah Remote (V2)** | Remote dashboard, multi-project view, report generation |\n\n**MVP capability set derived from journeys:** Project setup, unit board with statuses, multi-trade tagging, board filtering, one-tap claiming, voice commands, real-time sync, block status with notes, shareable no-auth link, superintendent overview.\n\n## Web App Specific Requirements\n\n### Project-Type Overview\n\nTaskFlow is a mobile-first Progressive Web App (PWA) built as a Single Page Application. The architecture prioritizes real-time data synchronization, offline resilience, and extreme interaction simplicity over traditional web app concerns like SEO, complex routing, or rich content rendering.\n\n### Browser & Device Matrix\n\n| Target | Priority | Minimum Version | Notes |\n|--------|----------|----------------|-------|\n| Chrome Android | **Primary** | Last 2 major versions | 70%+ of crew devices |\n| Safari iOS | Secondary | iOS 15+ | iPhone users on crew |\n| Chrome Desktop | Setup only | Last 2 major versions | Mike's project setup flow |\n| Safari Desktop | Setup only | Last 2 major versions | Fallback for Mac users |\n\n**Not supported:** Internet Explorer, Firefox Mobile, Opera Mini, or any browser without WebSocket support.\n\n### Responsive Design Strategy\n\n- **Mobile (320px–480px)**: Primary design target. Single-column board layout. All interactions reachable with one thumb. Minimum tap target: 48x48px.\n- **Tablet (481px–1024px)**: Enhanced board density — two-column unit grid. Superintendent monitoring view.\n- **Desktop (1025px+)**: Project setup and administration. Multi-column board with expanded unit detail. Not optimized for daily worker use.\n\n### Performance Targets\n\nSee Non-Functional Requirements for specific performance targets.\n\n### SEO Strategy\n\n**Not applicable.** TaskFlow is a private coordination tool accessed via direct shared links. No public-facing pages, no search engine indexing needed. `robots.txt` disallows all crawling. Board URLs use opaque project IDs, not human-readable slugs.\n\n### Accessibility Approach\n\nTaskFlow's accessibility strategy is driven by its hostile-environment context rather than traditional WCAG compliance:\n\n- **Voice-first interaction**: Primary accessibility feature — hands-free operation via voice commands (\"Claim Unit 12\", \"Mark done\")\n- **High contrast mode**: Default UI uses high-contrast colors readable in direct sunlight\n- **Large touch targets**: Minimum 48x48px tap areas; claim checkboxes oversized for gloved/dirty hands\n- **Minimal text dependency**: Status communicated through color + icon + position, not text-heavy UI\n- **Screen reader compatible**: Semantic HTML with ARIA labels for standard assistive technology support\n- **No fine motor requirements**: No drag-and-drop, no small buttons, no hover states required\n\n### Real-Time Architecture Considerations\n\n- **Protocol**: WebSocket with SSE fallback for environments blocking WebSocket upgrades\n- **Reconnection**: Automatic reconnect with exponential backoff; visual indicator when disconnected\n- **Conflict resolution**: Last-write-wins for status changes; optimistic UI with server reconciliation\n- **State management**: Client maintains local board state; server pushes deltas, not full board refreshes\n\n### Implementation Considerations\n\n- **PWA manifest**: Configured for standalone display mode, portrait orientation lock on mobile\n- **Service worker**: Cache-first for static assets, network-first for board data with offline fallback\n- **No authentication in MVP**: Board access via shareable URL with opaque project ID (security through obscurity, acceptable for MVP)\n- **Voice integration**: Web Speech API for voice commands; fallback to manual input if browser doesn't support or user denies mic permission\n\n## Functional Requirements\n\n### Board Management\n\n- FR1: Superintendent can create a new project board by defining a project name\n- FR2: Superintendent can define units (rooms/apartments) on a project board with unit identifiers\n- FR3: Superintendent can assign one or more trade types to each unit\n- FR4: Superintendent can set unit status (available, in-progress, blocked, complete)\n- FR5: Superintendent can generate a shareable link for the project board\n- FR6: Superintendent can edit project board configuration after creation (add/remove units, modify trades)\n\n### Task Visibility\n\n- FR7: Any crew member can view the full project board showing all units and their current statuses\n- FR8: Crew member can filter the board to show only units matching their trade(s)\n- FR9: Crew member can see which units are available, in-progress, blocked, or complete at a glance via visual status indicators\n- FR10: Crew member can see who has claimed a unit currently in-progress\n- FR11: Crew member can view notes attached to a blocked unit explaining the blocking reason\n\n### Task Coordination\n\n- FR12: Crew member can claim an available unit with a single interaction\n- FR13: Crew member can mark their claimed unit as complete\n- FR14: Crew member can release a claimed unit back to available status\n- FR15: Crew member can add a status note when updating a unit (e.g., \"paint drying, back tomorrow\")\n- FR16: System prevents multiple crew members from claiming the same unit simultaneously\n- FR16a: Crew member can flag a unit as blocked and provide a reason (e.g., \"drywall not finished\", \"no power to unit\")\n- FR16b: Crew member can remove a block on a unit when the blocking condition is resolved\n- FR16c: System records who set and who cleared each block for accountability\n\n### Real-Time Communication\n\n- FR17: All board changes propagate to all connected clients within the performance target window\n- FR18: System displays a visual indicator when a client loses server connection\n- FR19: System automatically reconnects when connection is restored and syncs board state\n- FR20: System maintains a viewable cached board state when offline\n- FR21: System resolves conflicting simultaneous updates using last-write-wins with optimistic UI\n\n### Voice Interaction\n\n- FR22: Crew member can issue voice commands to claim a unit (e.g., \"Claim Unit 12\")\n- FR23: Crew member can issue voice commands to update unit status (e.g., \"Mark done\")\n- FR24: Crew member can add voice-dictated notes to a unit\n- FR25: System provides visual confirmation of recognized voice commands before executing\n- FR26: System gracefully degrades to manual input when voice recognition is unavailable or denied\n\n### Crew Identity\n\n- FR27: First-time visitor can establish a lightweight identity by entering their name and selecting their trade(s)\n- FR28: System persists crew member identity locally across browser sessions\n- FR29: System associates crew member actions (claims, completions) with their identity\n- FR30: Crew member can update their name or trade associations\n\n### Superintendent Operations\n\n- FR31: Superintendent can view an overview of all units with aggregated progress statistics\n- FR32: Superintendent can mark any unit as blocked and attach a reason note\n- FR33: Superintendent can unblock a unit, returning it to available status\n- FR34: Superintendent can reassign or unclaim a unit currently claimed by a crew member\n- FR35: Superintendent can see a summary of crew activity (who claimed what, when)\n- FR35a: Superintendent can view a log of block/unblock actions by crew members\n\n## Non-Functional Requirements\n\n### Performance\n\n- NFR1: Board state changes propagate to all connected clients within 2 seconds under normal network conditions\n- NFR2: Initial board load completes within 3 seconds on a 3G mobile connection\n- NFR3: Voice command recognition completes within 1 second of utterance end\n- NFR4: UI interactions (tap to claim, status change) provide visual feedback within 100ms\n- NFR5: System supports 20 concurrent users on a single project board without performance degradation\n\n### Reliability & Availability\n\n- NFR6: Application remains fully functional for board viewing and local actions when network connection is lost\n- NFR7: System automatically detects connection loss and displays status indicator within 5 seconds\n- NFR8: System automatically reconnects and syncs board state when connectivity is restored, without user intervention\n- NFR9: No board data is lost during network interruptions — all local actions queue and sync on reconnection\n- NFR10: System resolves sync conflicts deterministically (last-write-wins) without requiring user intervention\n- NFR11: Application recovers gracefully from browser refresh, preserving user identity and board state\n\n### Accessibility (Construction Environment)\n\n- NFR12: All interactive elements have minimum touch target size of 48x48px (gloved finger operation)\n- NFR13: Board status indicators are distinguishable by both color AND shape/icon (sunlight washout, color blindness)\n- NFR14: All critical information maintains readable contrast ratio in direct sunlight (minimum 7:1 contrast)\n- NFR15: Voice interaction provides an alternative input path for all primary actions (claim, complete, block, note)\n- NFR16: Application is fully usable in portrait orientation on devices 5\" and larger\n- NFR17: No interaction requires two-handed operation\n\n### Security & Data Integrity\n\n- NFR18: Board data transmitted over HTTPS (TLS 1.2+)\n- NFR19: Shareable project links use unguessable tokens (minimum 128-bit entropy)\n- NFR20: System prevents race conditions on unit claiming (server-authoritative state)\n\n### Scalability (Growth Path)\n\n- NFR21: Architecture supports horizontal scaling to accommodate multi-project, multi-crew usage in V2 without fundamental redesign\n- NFR22: Data model supports future addition of user authentication layer without migration of existing board data\n\"\"\"\n\n# Write the file\noutput_path = '/a0/usr/projects/bmad_and_agent-zero/_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/prd.md'\nwith open(output_path, 'w') as f:\n    f.write(doc.lstrip())\n\nprint(f\"Written {len(doc.strip())} characters to {output_path}\")\nprint(\"Done.\")"
    }
}

## What's Done
Phase 1 (Analysis) is COMPLETE. The Product Brief for "TaskFlow" is finished:
→ _bmad-output/planning-artifacts/product-brief-taskflow-2026-02-26.md

All 6 steps completed: Vision, Users, Metrics, MVP Scope, Completion.

## What is TaskFlow
A lightweight, mobile-first task coordination tool for small construction remodeling crews (5 trades, 15 workers, 25 units). Replaces "shouting across the site" with a digital board where workers self-assign tasks by unit and trade.

Key personas:
- Carlos (Trades Worker) — needs to see available tasks, claim with checkbox or voice command
- Mike (Site Super) — needs real-time overview, stop being the human dispatcher  
- Sarah (Remote PM) — needs status visibility without calling Mike (V2 feature)

MVP features: Unit Board, Multi-Trade Tagging, Board Filtering (remembers preferences), Task Claiming, Live Board, Voice Commands.

## What's Next
Please read the completed product brief, then start the BMAD Phase 2 workflow: create-prd (Product Requirements Document).

The PRD workflow is located at:
_bmad/bmm/workflows/2-plan-workflows/create-prd/

Follow the BMAD workflow steps as a facilitator — read each step file fully before acting, use the collaborative discovery approach (don't generate content without my input), and present A/P/C menus at each step.

## 🎯 Changelog

### v0.9.8 - Skills, UI Redesign & Git projects
[Release video](https://youtu.be/NV7s78yn6DY)

- Skills
    - Skills System replacing the legacy Instruments with a new `SKILL.md` standard for structured, portable agent capabilities.
    - Built-in skills, and UI support for importing and listing skills
- Real-time WebSocket infrastructure replacing the polling-based approach for UI state synchronization
- UI Redesign
    - Process groups to visually group agent actions with expand/collapse support
    - Timestamps, steps count and execution time with tool-specific badges
    - Step detail modals with key-value and raw JSON display
    - Collapsible responses with show more/less and copy buttons on code blocks and tables
    - Message queue system allowing users to queue messages while the agent is still processing
    - In-browser file editor for viewing and editing files without leaving the UI
    - Welcome screen redesign with info and warning banners for connection security, missing API keys, and system resources
    - Scheduler redesign with standalone modal, separate task list, detail and editor components, and project support
    - Smooth response rendering and scroll stabilization across chat, terminals, and image viewer
    - Chat width setting and reworked preferences panel
    - Image viewer improvements with scroll support and expanded viewer
    - Redesigned sidebar with reusable dropdown component and streamlined buttons
    - Inline button confirmations for critical actions
    - Improved login design and new logout button
    - File browser enhanced with rename and file actions dropdown
- Git projects
    - Git-based projects with clone authentication for public and private repositories
- Four new LLM providers: CometAPI, Z.AI, Moonshot AI, and AWS Bedrock
- Microsoft Dev Tunnels integration for secure remote access
- User data migration to `/usr` directory for cleaner separation of user and system files
- Subagents system with configurable agent profiles for different roles
- Memory operations offloaded to deferred tasks for better performance
- Environment variables can now configure settings via `A0_SET_*` prefix in `.env`
- Automatic migration with overwrite support for `.env`, scheduler, knowledge, and legacy directories
- Projects support extended to MCP, A2A, and external API
- Workdir outside project support for more flexible file organization
- Agent number tracking in backend and responses for multi-agent identification
- Many bug fixes and stability improvements across the UI, MCP tools, scheduler, uploads, and WebSocket handling


### v0.9.7 - Projects

- New Wait tool

- Support for multiple documents at once in document_query_tool
- Improved context on interventions
- Openrouter embedding support
- Frontend components refactor and polishing
- SSH metadata output fix
- Support for windows powershell in local TTY utility
- More efficient selective streaming for LLMs
- UI output length limit improvements

### v0.9.6 - Memory Dashboard

- Memory Management Dashboard
- Kali update
- Python update + dual installation
- Browser Use update

- Github Copilot provider support

# Product Brief: TaskFlow

## Executive Summary

TaskFlow is a lightweight, mobile-first task coordination tool designed for small-to-mid construction remodeling crews. It replaces the current "shouting across the site" coordination method with a simple digital board where trades workers can see available tasks by unit, self-assign work, and track what others are doing — all through quick checkbox taps or voice commands.

Designed for crews of 5–25 workers across multiple trades and units, TaskFlow eliminates the most common remodeling bottleneck: work stalling because one trade didn't know another trade needed their input first.

---

## Core Vision

### Problem Statement

On construction remodeling projects with multiple units, there is no simple way to track who is working where, what trade they represent, or what tasks are pending. Coordination relies entirely on the site superintendent's memory and verbal communication, creating a single point of failure that leads to blocked work, idle workers, and missed dependencies between trades.

### Problem Impact

- **Work stalls** when Trade A doesn't know Trade B needed to finish first
- **Workers sit idle** because they don't know which units need their trade
- **Site super is overwhelmed** mentally tracking 15 workers × 25 units × 5 trades
- **No visibility** — nobody has a real-time picture of site status

### Why Existing Solutions Fall Short

Enterprise construction management platforms (Procore, Fieldwire, Buildertrend) are designed for general contractors managing million-dollar projects with complex compliance requirements. For a 15-person remodeling crew, they are overkill — too expensive, too complex, and too slow to adopt. Workers need something they can use in 30 seconds with dirty hands, not a project management suite.

### Proposed Solution

A mobile-first task board organized by **unit** and **trade**, where:
- Workers see which units need their trade at a glance
- Tasks can be claimed with a **checkbox tap** or **voice command**
- Everyone sees who is working where in real-time
- The site super gets an instant overview without being the bottleneck
- Trade dependencies are visible (e.g., "Plumbing rough-in needed before drywall")

### Key Differentiators

- **Dead simple**: Designed for speed — checkbox or voice, nothing else needed
- **Trade-aware**: Workers are tagged by trade, tasks are filtered by trade
- **Unit-centric**: Organized around physical workspaces, not abstract project hierarchies
- **Built for the crew, not the office**: No training required, works with dirty hands
- **Real-time coordination**: Eliminates the site super as a communication bottleneck

---

## Target Users

### Primary Users

#### Carlos — The Trades Worker

**Profile:** Electrician, 10 years in the trade. Shows up at 7am, checks in with the super, then figures out where to go. Has a smartphone in his pocket but has zero patience for complicated apps — if it takes more than 30 seconds, he's not using it.

**Current Experience:** Arrives on site, finds Mike (the super), waits for direction. If Mike is busy with another trade, Carlos either waits or guesses. Sometimes he walks to a unit only to find another trade is already there, or the prerequisite work isn't done yet.

**Pain Points:**
- Wastes time waiting for direction or walking to the wrong unit
- No visibility into what other trades are doing
- Doesn't know when his work is blocked by another trade's incomplete task

**Success Vision:** Opens his phone at 7am, sees that Unit 12 needs electrical rough-in and it's ready for him. Taps it, claims it, walks straight there. No waiting for Mike.

---

#### Mike — The Site Superintendent

**Profile:** First one on site, last one to leave. Walks the site every morning to assess status, then spends half his day answering "where should I go?" from 15 workers across 5 trades. Keeps the entire project state in his head.

**Current Experience:** Mentally tracks which units are ready for which trades, who's working where, and what's blocked. When two workers ask him questions at the same time, something gets dropped. By afternoon, he's exhausted from coordination, not from building.

**Pain Points:**
- He IS the coordination system — single point of failure
- Can't be everywhere at once on a 25-unit project
- No way to communicate status changes to everyone simultaneously
- Spends more time directing traffic than managing quality

**Success Vision:** Glances at TaskFlow and sees the whole site at once — who's where, what's done, what's blocked. Workers self-assign, and Mike focuses on quality and problem-solving instead of being a human dispatcher.

---

### Secondary Users

#### Sarah — The Remote Project Manager

**Profile:** Oversees multiple remodeling projects from the office. Currently relies on end-of-day calls with Mike to get status updates. Needs to report progress to property owners and stakeholders.

**Current Experience:** Calls Mike at 4pm, interrupts his work, gets a verbal summary that she scribbles into a spreadsheet. Has no real-time visibility and can't answer stakeholder questions during the day.

**Success Vision:** Opens TaskFlow from her laptop, sees real-time progress across all 25 units — which are complete, in-progress, or blocked. Can generate a status snapshot for the property owner without bothering Mike.

---

### User Journey

| Phase | Carlos (Worker) | Mike (Super) |
|-------|-----------------|---------------|
| **Discovery** | Mike tells the crew "we're using this app now" | Hears about it from PM or finds it himself |
| **Onboarding** | Opens link on phone, picks his trade, sees the board | Sets up units and initial tasks in 10 minutes |
| **Core Usage** | Checks board at 7am, taps a unit, claims a task | Reviews board, updates blocked tasks, monitors progress |
| **Aha! Moment** | Walks straight to Unit 12 without asking anyone | Realizes he hasn't been asked "where should I go?" all morning |
| **Routine** | Part of the morning ritual — coffee, phone, TaskFlow, go | Starts the day reviewing the board instead of walking the site |

---

## Success Metrics

### User Success Metrics

| Metric | Target | How We Measure |
|--------|--------|----------------|
| **"Where should I go?" questions eliminated** | 90% reduction by end of week 1 | Mike tracks daily interruption count |
| **Time from arrival to productive work** | Workers start 15 minutes earlier | Compare pre/post TaskFlow morning routines |
| **Blocked work incidents** | Eliminate 1+ per week | Track tasks marked "blocked" that were caught before a worker showed up |
| **Worker self-assignment rate** | 80%+ of tasks self-assigned (not directed by Mike) | TaskFlow logs: claimed vs. assigned-by-super |

### Business Objectives

| Objective | Target | Timeframe |
|-----------|--------|-----------|
| **Crew adoption** | 80% of workers actively using TaskFlow | By day 3 of rollout |
| **Organic usage** | Mike stops reminding workers to check the app | By end of week 1 |
| **Daily time savings** | 30 min/worker/day (7.5 hours total crew savings) | Measured at 2-week mark |
| **Coordination overhead** | Mike spends <20% of day on "traffic directing" (down from ~50%) | By end of week 2 |

### Key Performance Indicators

| KPI | Definition | Leading/Lagging |
|-----|------------|-----------------|
| **Daily Active Users** | Workers who open TaskFlow and interact at least once | Leading |
| **Task Claim Rate** | % of available tasks claimed within 30 min of posting | Leading |
| **Blocked Task Detection** | Tasks flagged as blocked before a worker arrives at the unit | Leading |
| **Site Throughput** | Units completed per week (pre vs. post TaskFlow) | Lagging |
| **Super Satisfaction** | Mike's weekly rating: "How much time did TaskFlow save you?" | Lagging |

---

## MVP Scope

### Core Features (MVP — Must Ship)

| # | Feature | What It Does | Why It's Essential |
|---|---------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Unit Board** | Grid/list of all units with status: Available, In-Progress, Done, Blocked | This IS the product — the single source of truth replacing Mike's brain |
| 2 | **Trade Tagging (Multi-Trade)** | Workers select one or more trades on first login (e.g., Laborer + Painter). Workers can belong to multiple trades simultaneously. | Reflects real-world reality — many workers carry multiple trades |
| 3 | **Board Filtering** | Filter the board by trade. Default view shows all tasks for trades the worker belongs to. Filter preferences are remembered per worker. | Carlos sees electrical tasks by default; a Laborer/Painter sees both. No information overload. |
| 4 | **Task Claiming** | Tap a checkbox to claim a unit task; your name and trade appear on the board | The core interaction — replaces "Hey Mike, where should I go?" |
| 5 | **Live Board** | Real-time updates — everyone sees who's working where as it happens | Eliminates the information vacuum; no more walking to an occupied unit |
| 6 | **Voice Commands** | "Claim Unit 12" / "Mark Unit 7 electrical done" — hands-free operation | Non-negotiable for dirty-hands, job-site reality |

### Out of Scope for MVP

| Feature | Why It's Deferred | When It Comes |
|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Remote PM Dashboard** (Sarah's view) | Core value is on-site coordination; remote access is a nice-to-have | V2 — after validating on-site adoption |
| **Automatic Dependency Blocking** | Complex logic; manual "blocked" status is sufficient for MVP | V2 — once we understand real dependency patterns |
| **Historical Reports & Analytics** | Not needed to solve the daily coordination problem | V2 — when PMs and owners want progress tracking |
| **Multi-Site Support** | MVP targets one crew on one project | V3 — when scaling to multiple crews |
| **Photo Documentation** | Useful but not core to "who's where doing what" | V2 — natural extension of task completion |
| **User Accounts / Auth** | MVP can use simple name + trade selection (no passwords) | V2 — required for multi-site and data security |

### MVP Success Criteria

| Gate | Metric | Threshold |
|------|--------|-----------|
| **Adoption** | % of crew using TaskFlow daily | ≥ 80% by day 3 |
| **Self-Service** | Tasks claimed without Mike directing | ≥ 80% of daily tasks |
| **Time Savings** | Reduction in morning "where do I go?" wait time | ≥ 15 min/worker/day |
| **Coordination** | Mike's time spent directing traffic | < 20% of his day |
| **Go/No-Go** | If all gates met → proceed to V2 features | Measured at 2-week mark |

### Future Vision

**The Big Picture: Most remodeling crews in the city use TaskFlow.**

| Phase | Milestone | Key Additions |
|-------|-----------|---------------|
| **V1 (MVP)** | One crew, one site, core coordination | Unit board, multi-trade tagging, filtering, claiming, voice, live updates |
| **V2** | Power features for the same crew | PM dashboard, dependency tracking, photo docs, reporting |
| **V3** | Multi-site, multi-crew | User accounts, site switching, GC oversight view |
| **V4** | City-wide platform | Crew marketplace, trade scheduling across projects, reputation system |

The MVP is the **wedge** — prove it works for one crew, then expand outward.

---
name: create-prd
description: 'Create a PRD from scratch. Use when the user says "lets create a product requirements document" or "I want to create a new PRD"'
main_config: '{project-root}/_bmad/bmm/config.yaml'
nextStep: './steps-c/step-01-init.md'
---

# PRD Create Workflow

**Goal:** Create comprehensive PRDs through structured workflow facilitation.

**Your Role:** Product-focused PM facilitator collaborating with an expert peer.

You will continue to operate with your given name, identity, and communication_style, merged with the details of this role description.

## WORKFLOW ARCHITECTURE

This uses **step-file architecture** for disciplined execution:

### Core Principles

- **Micro-file Design**: Each step is a self contained instruction file that is a part of an overall workflow that must be followed exactly
- **Just-In-Time Loading**: Only the current step file is in memory - never load future step files until told to do so
- **Sequential Enforcement**: Sequence within the step files must be completed in order, no skipping or optimization allowed
- **State Tracking**: Document progress in output file frontmatter using `stepsCompleted` array when a workflow produces a document
- **Append-Only Building**: Build documents by appending content as directed to the output file

### Step Processing Rules

1. **READ COMPLETELY**: Always read the entire step file before taking any action
2. **FOLLOW SEQUENCE**: Execute all numbered sections in order, never deviate
3. **WAIT FOR INPUT**: If a menu is presented, halt and wait for user selection
4. **CHECK CONTINUATION**: If the step has a menu with Continue as an option, only proceed to next step when user selects 'C' (Continue)
5. **SAVE STATE**: Update `stepsCompleted` in frontmatter before loading next step
6. **LOAD NEXT**: When directed, read fully and follow the next step file

### Critical Rules (NO EXCEPTIONS)

- 🛑 **NEVER** load multiple step files simultaneously
- 📖 **ALWAYS** read entire step file before execution
- 🚫 **NEVER** skip steps or optimize the sequence
- 💾 **ALWAYS** update frontmatter of output files when writing the final output for a specific step
- 🎯 **ALWAYS** follow the exact instructions in the step file
- ⏸️ **ALWAYS** halt at menus and wait for user input
- 📋 **NEVER** create mental todo lists from future steps

## INITIALIZATION SEQUENCE

### 1. Configuration Loading

Load and read full config from {main_config} and resolve:

- `project_name`, `output_folder`, `planning_artifacts`, `user_name`
- `communication_language`, `document_output_language`, `user_skill_level`
- `date` as system-generated current datetime

✅ YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT In your Agent communication style with the configured `{communication_language}`.

### 2. Route to Create Workflow

"**Create Mode: Creating a new PRD from scratch.**"

Read fully and follow: `{nextStep}` (steps-c/step-01-init.md)

Product Vision:

Long-term Vision:
Scaling from one crew to city-wide adoption via a V1-V4 roadmap including photo documentation and multi-site support.

Key Personas:
1. Mike (Site Superintendent): Site-wide overview.
2. Carlos (Trades Worker): Task visibility.
3. Sarah (Remote PM): Real-time progress visibility.

Current Status (2026-02-26):

- Active Persona: 'Mary' (Senior Business Analyst).
- Artifacts: Product Brief saved at `/a0/usr/projects/bmad_and_agent-zero/_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/product-brief-taskflow-2026-02-26.md`.
- Next Step: Execute `create-prd` workflow (Product Requirements Document) located at `_bmad/bmm/workflows/2-plan-workflows/create-prd/`.

Success Metrics:
- 90% reduction in coordination questions.
- 15-minute faster worker start times.

kvps.solutions:
# Problem
Integrating the BMAD (Breakthrough Method for Agile AI-Driven Development) framework with Agent Zero to build 'TaskFlow' (construction safety app), while maintaining project state and context across different AI conversation sessions.

# Solution
1. **Environment & Terminal Setup**:
   - Install BMAD in the project directory. 
   - **Terminal Fix**: Use a PTY wrapper to avoid 'RangeError': `stty cols 120 rows 40 2>/dev/null; script -qc 'COLUMNS=120 npx bmad-method install --directory . --tools none --yes' /dev/null`.

2. **State Tracking & Persistence**:
   - **Output Directory**: Use `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/` to store evolving documents (Product Brief/PRD).
   - **Frontmatter Metadata**: Use YAML frontmatter in markdown files to track progress (e.g., `stepsCompleted: [1, 2, 3]`) and metadata (date, author, input documents).
   - **A/P/C System**: Use the BMAD menu: `[A] Advanced Elicitation`, `[P] Party Mode`, or `[C] Continue` to save progress.

3. **Phase 1: Analysis Workflow Execution**:
   - **Persona**: Assume 'Business Analyst' (e.g., 'Mary'). Conduct interviews; do not generate autonomously.
   - **Step 1-6**: Progress from Initialization (Step 1) through Vision, User Discovery (Personas like 'Carlos' and 'Mike'), Success Metrics, and MVP Scope (Unit Board, Voice Commands) to Completion.
   - **Read First**: Always read the full step file (e.g., `step-01-init.md`) before acting.

4. **Session Handoff Protocol**:
   - Generate a structured 'Continuation Prompt' for new sessions including:
     - Absolute paths to project and BMAD directories.
     - Summary of TaskFlow project and key personas.
     - Current status (e.g., Phase 1 Analysis Complete).
     - Path to the next workflow (e.g., `_bmad/bmm/workflows/2-plan-workflows/create-prd/`).
     - Instructions for the new agent to act as a facilitator following the A/P/C system.

5. **Next Steps**: Transition to the `create-prd` workflow for user stories and technical specs.

bash>cat /a0/usr/projects/bmad_and_agent-zero/_bmad/bmm/workflows/2-plan-workflows/create-prd/steps-c/ste...

---
name: 'step-01-init'
description: 'Initialize the PRD workflow by detecting continuation state and setting up the document'

# File References
nextStepFile: './step-02-discovery.md'
continueStepFile: './step-01b-continue.md'
outputFile: '{planning_artifacts}/prd.md'

# Template Reference
prdTemplate: '../templates/prd-template.md'
---

# Step 1: Workflow Initialization

**Progress: Step 1 of 11** - Next: Project Discovery

## STEP GOAL:

Initialize the PRD workflow by detecting continuation state, discovering input documents, and setting up the document structure for collaborative product requirement discovery.

## MANDATORY EXECUTION RULES (READ FIRST):

### Universal Rules:

- 🛑 NEVER generate content without user input
- 📖 CRITICAL: Read the complete step file before taking any action
- 🔄 CRITICAL: When loading next step with 'C', ensure entire file is read
- 📋 YOU ARE A FACILITATOR, not a content generator
- ✅ YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT In your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`

### Role Reinforcement:

- ✅ You are a product-focused PM facilitator collaborating with an expert peer
- ✅ If you already have been given a name, communication_style and persona, continue to use those while playing this new role
- ✅ We engage in collaborative dialogue, not command-response
- ✅ You bring structured thinking and facilitation skills, while the user brings domain expertise and product vision

### Step-Specific Rules:

- 🎯 Focus only on initialization and setup - no content generation yet
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to look ahead to future steps or assume knowledge from them
- 💬 Approach: Systematic setup with clear reporting to user
- 🚪 Detect existing workflow state and handle continuation properly

## EXECUTION PROTOCOLS:

- 🎯 Show your analysis of current state before taking any action
- 💾 Initialize document structure and update frontmatter appropriately
- Update frontmatter: add this step name to the end of the steps completed array (it should be the first entry in the steps array since this is step 1)
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to load next step until user selects 'C' (Continue)

## CONTEXT BOUNDARIES:

- Available context: Variables from workflow.md are available in memory
- Focus: Workflow initialization and document setup only
- Limits: Don't assume knowledge from other steps or create content yet
- Dependencies: Configuration loaded from workflow.md initialization

## Sequence of Instructions (Do not deviate, skip, or optimize)

### 1. Check for Existing Workflow State

First, check if the output document already exists:

**Workflow State Detection:**

- Look for file at `{outputFile}`
- If exists, read the complete file including frontmatter
- If not exists, this is a fresh workflow

### 2. Handle Continuation (If Document Exists)

If the document exists and has frontmatter with `stepsCompleted` BUT `step-11-complete` is NOT in the list, follow the Continuation Protocol since the document is incomplete:

**Continuation Protocol:**

- **STOP immediately** and load `{continueStepFile}`
- Do not proceed with any initialization tasks
- Let step-01b handle all continuation logic
- This is an auto-proceed situation - no user choice needed

### 3. Fresh Workflow Setup (If No Document)

If no document exists or no `stepsCompleted` in frontmatter:

#### A. Input Document Discovery

Discover and load context documents using smart discovery. Documents can be in the following locations:
- {planning_artifacts}/**
- {output_folder}/**
- {product_knowledge}/**
- docs/**

Also - when searching - documents can be a single markdown file, or a folder with an index and multiple files. For Example, if searching for `*foo*.md` and not found, also search for a folder called *foo*/index.md (which indicates sharded content)

Try to discover the following:
- Product Brief (`*brief*.md`)
- Research Documents (`/*research*.md`)
- Project Documentation (generally multiple documents might be found for this in the `{product_knowledge}` or `docs` folder.)
- Project Context (`**/project-context.md`)

<critical>Confirm what you have found with the user, along with asking if the user wants to provide anything else. Only after this confirmation will you proceed to follow the loading rules</critical>

**Loading Rules:**

- Load ALL discovered files completely that the user confirmed or provided (no offset/limit)
- If there is a project context, whatever is relevant should try to be biased in the remainder of this whole workflow process
- For sharded folders, load ALL files to get complete picture, using the index first to potentially know the potential of each document
- index.md is a guide to what's relevant whenever available
- Track all successfully loaded files in frontmatter `inputDocuments` array

#### B. Create Initial Document

**Document Setup:**

- Copy the template from `{prdTemplate}` to `{outputFile}`
- Initialize frontmatter with proper structure including inputDocuments array.

#### C. Present Initialization Results

**Setup Report to User:**

"Welcome {{user_name}}! I've set up your PRD workspace for {{project_name}}.

**Document Setup:**

- Created: `{outputFile}` from template
- Initialized frontmatter with workflow state

**Input Documents Discovered:**

- Product briefs: {{briefCount}} files {if briefCount > 0}✓ loaded{else}(none found){/if}
- Research: {{researchCount}} files {if researchCount > 0}✓ loaded{else}(none found){/if}
- Brainstorming: {{brainstormingCount}} files {if brainstormingCount > 0}✓ loaded{else}(none found){/if}
- Project docs: {{projectDocsCount}} files {if projectDocsCount > 0}✓ loaded (brownfield project){else}(none found - greenfield project){/if}

**Files loaded:** {list of specific file names or "No additional documents found"}

{if projectDocsCount > 0}
📋 **Note:** This is a **brownfield project**. Your existing project documentation has been loaded. In the next step, I'll ask specifically about what new features or changes you want to add to your existing system.
{/if}

Do you have any other documents you'd like me to include, or shall we continue to the next step?"

### 4. Present MENU OPTIONS

Display menu after setup report:

"[C] Continue - Save this and move to Project Discovery (Step 2 of 11)"

#### Menu Handling Logic:

- IF C: Update output file frontmatter, adding this step name to the end of the list of stepsCompleted, then read fully and follow: {nextStepFile}
- IF user provides additional files: Load them, update inputDocuments and documentCounts, redisplay report
- IF user asks questions: Answer and redisplay menu

#### EXECUTION RULES:

- ALWAYS halt and wait for user input after presenting menu
- ONLY proceed to next step when user selects 'C'

## CRITICAL STEP COMPLETION NOTE

ONLY WHEN [C continue option] is selected and [frontmatter properly updated with this step added to stepsCompleted and documentCounts], will you then read fully and follow: `{nextStepFile}` to begin project discovery.

---

## 🚨 SYSTEM SUCCESS/FAILURE METRICS

### ✅ SUCCESS:

- Existing workflow detected and properly handed off to step-01b
- Fresh workflow initialized with template and proper frontmatter
- Input documents discovered and loaded using sharded-first logic
- All discovered files tracked in frontmatter `inputDocuments`
- User clearly informed of brownfield vs greenfield status
- Menu presented and user input handled correctly
- Frontmatter updated with this step name added to stepsCompleted before proceeding

### ❌ SYSTEM FAILURE:

- Proceeding with fresh initialization when existing workflow exists
- Not updating frontmatter with discovered input documents
- **Not storing document counts in frontmatter**
- Creating document without proper template structure
- Not checking sharded folders first before whole files
- Not reporting discovered documents to user clearly
- Proceeding without user selecting 'C' (Continue)

**Master Rule:** Skipping steps, optimizing sequences, or not following exact instructions is FORBIDDEN and constitutes SYSTEM FAILURE.

kvps.memories:
User is developing 'TaskFlow', a mobile-first task coordination tool for construction remodeling crews (5 trades, 15 workers, 25 units) using the BMAD (Breakthrough Method for Agile AI-Driven Development) framework.

Project: TaskFlow - Construction Site Workspace Tracker

Environment & Framework:
- Location: `/a0/usr/projects/bmad_and_agent-zero` (9p mount).
- Framework: BMAD (Breakthrough Method for Agile AI-Driven Development) v6.0.3 via 'bmm' module.
- Tech Stack: Node.js (v20+), Python Flask, SQLite, HTML/CSS/JS.
- Agent Zero Integration: Specialized personas using custom skills (bmad_framework, bmad_reviewer, voice_mapper) and automation scripts (bmad_bridge.py, bt-init.py).

Product Vision:
A mobile-first construction site task coordination tool for remodeling crews to eliminate coordination bottlenecks. Features include trade dependency tracking, mini Kanban boards, and 'dirty hands' optimized UI.

Long-term Vision:
Scaling from one crew to city-wide adoption via a V1-V4 roadmap including photo documentation and multi-site support.

Key Personas:
1. Mike (Site Superintendent): Site-wide overview.
2. Carlos (Trades Worker): Task visibility.
3. Sarah (Remote PM): Real-time progress visibility.

Current Status (2026-02-26):
- Phase: Analysis Phase COMPLETE.
- Active Persona: 'Mary' (Senior Business Analyst).
- Artifacts: Product Brief saved at `/a0/usr/projects/bmad_and_agent-zero/_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/product-brief-taskflow-2026-02-26.md`.
- Next Step: Execute `create-prd` workflow (Product Requirements Document) located at `_bmad/bmm/workflows/2-plan-workflows/create-prd/`.

Success Metrics:
- 90% reduction in coordination questions.
- 15-minute faster worker start times.
- 30 minutes saved per worker per day.
- 80% crew adoption by day 3.

The BMAD 'Analysis' phase is complete; the Product Brief is saved at `/a0/usr/projects/bmad_and_agent-zero/_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/product-brief-taskflow-2026-02-26.md`.

# Problem
Integrating the BMAD (Breakthrough Method for Agile AI-Driven Development) framework with Agent Zero to build 'TaskFlow' (construction safety app), while maintaining project state and context across different AI conversation sessions.

# Solution
1. **Environment & Terminal Setup**:
   - Install BMAD in the project directory. 
   - **Terminal Fix**: Use a PTY wrapper to avoid 'RangeError': `stty cols 120 rows 40 2>/dev/null; script -qc 'COLUMNS=120 npx bmad-method install --directory . --tools none --yes' /dev/null`.

2. **State Tracking & Persistence**:
   - **Output Directory**: Use `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/` to store evolving documents (Product Brief/PRD).
   - **Frontmatter Metadata**: Use YAML frontmatter in markdown files to track progress (e.g., `stepsCompleted: [1, 2, 3]`) and metadata (date, author, input documents).
   - **A/P/C System**: Use the BMAD menu: `[A] Advanced Elicitation`, `[P] Party Mode`, or `[C] Continue` to save progress.

3. **Phase 1: Analysis Workflow Execution**:
   - **Persona**: Assume 'Business Analyst' (e.g., 'Mary'). Conduct interviews; do not generate autonomously.
   - **Step 1-6**: Progress from Initialization (Step 1) through Vision, User Discovery (Personas like 'Carlos' and 'Mike'), Success Metrics, and MVP Scope (Unit Board, Voice Commands) to Completion.
   - **Read First**: Always read the full step file (e.g., `step-01-init.md`) before acting.

4. **Session Handoff Protocol**:
   - Generate a structured 'Continuation Prompt' for new sessions including:
     - Absolute paths to project and BMAD directories.
     - Summary of TaskFlow project and key personas.
     - Current status (e.g., Phase 1 Analysis Complete).
     - Path to the next workflow (e.g., `_bmad/bmm/workflows/2-plan-workflows/create-prd/`).
     - Instructions for the new agent to act as a facilitator following the A/P/C system.

5. **Next Steps**: Transition to the `create-prd` workflow for user stories and technical specs.

kvps.solutions:
# Problem
The user wants to identify and explore attached volumes and folder structures within the Agent Zero environment, specifically looking for project files mounted from a host system.

# Solution
1. **Filter Mount Points**: Use the terminal to list active mounts while filtering out system-specific virtual filesystems to isolate user-attached volumes: `mount | grep -v 'proc\|sys\|cgroup\|devpts\|mqueue\|shm\|tmpfs'`.
2. **Identify Host Mounts**: Look for specific filesystem types like `9p` (common for Windows/WSL integrations) or other external filesystems using `mount | grep 9p` to find host-shared directories.
3. **Inspect Directories**: Check common mount locations like `/a0/usr`, `/mnt`, `/data`, or `/volumes` using `ls -la` to verify accessible directories and files.
4. **Verify Project Context**: Explore specific project directories found in the mounts to understand the application structure by looking for indicators like `package.json`, `src/`, `.git/`, etc.

# Step 2: Project Discovery

**Progress: Step 2 of 13** - Next: Product Vision

## STEP GOAL:

Discover and classify the project - understand what type of product this is, what domain it operates in, and the project context (greenfield vs brownfield).

## MANDATORY EXECUTION RULES (READ FIRST):

### Universal Rules:

- 🛑 NEVER generate content without user input
- 📖 CRITICAL: Read the complete step file before taking any action
- 🔄 CRITICAL: When loading next step with 'C', ensure the entire file is read
- ✅ ALWAYS treat this as collaborative discovery between PM peers
- 📋 YOU ARE A FACILITATOR, not a content generator
- ✅ YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT In your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`

### Role Reinforcement:

- ✅ You are a product-focused PM facilitator collaborating with an expert peer
- ✅ We engage in collaborative dialogue, not command-response
- ✅ You bring structured thinking and facilitation skills, while the user brings domain expertise and product vision

### Step-Specific Rules:

- 🎯 Focus on classification and understanding - no content generation yet
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to generate executive summary or vision statements (that's next steps)
- 💬 APPROACH: Natural conversation to understand the project
- 🎯 LOAD classification data BEFORE starting discovery conversation

## EXECUTION PROTOCOLS:

- 🎯 Show your analysis before taking any action
- ⚠️ Present A/P/C menu after classification complete
- 💾 ONLY save classification to frontmatter when user chooses C (Continue)
- 📖 Update frontmatter, adding this step to the end of the list of stepsCompleted
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to load next step until C is selected

## CONTEXT BOUNDARIES:

- Current document and frontmatter from step 1 are available
- Input documents already loaded are in memory (product briefs, research, brainstorming, project docs)
- **Document counts available in frontmatter `documentCounts`**
- Classification CSV data will be loaded in this step only
- No executive summary or vision content yet (that's steps 2b and 2c)

## YOUR TASK:

Discover and classify the project through natural conversation:
- What type of product is this? (web app, API, mobile, etc.)
- What domain does it operate in? (healthcare, fintech, e-commerce, etc.)
- What's the project context? (greenfield new product vs brownfield existing system)
- How complex is this domain? (low, medium, high)

## DISCOVERY SEQUENCE:

### 1. Check Document State

Read the frontmatter from `{outputFile}` to get document counts:
- `briefCount` - Product briefs available
- `researchCount` - Research documents available
- `brainstormingCount` - Brainstorming docs available
- `projectDocsCount` - Existing project documentation

**Announce your understanding:**

"From step 1, I have loaded:
- Product briefs: {{briefCount}}
- Research: {{researchCount}}
- Brainstorming: {{brainstormingCount}}
- Project docs: {{projectDocsCount}}

{{if projectDocsCount > 0}}This is a brownfield project - I'll focus on understanding what you want to add or change.{{else}}This is a greenfield project - I'll help you define the full product vision.{{/if}}"

### 2. Load Classification Data

**Attempt subprocess data lookup:**

**Project Type Lookup:**
"Your task: Lookup data in {projectTypesCSV}

**Search criteria:**
- Find row where project_type matches {{detectedProjectType}}

**Return format:**
Return ONLY the matching row as a YAML-formatted object with these fields:
project_type, detection_signals

**Do NOT return the entire CSV - only the matching row.**"

**Domain Complexity Lookup:**
"Your task: Lookup data in {domainComplexityCSV}

**Search criteria:**
- Find row where domain matches {{detectedDomain}}

**Return format:**
Return ONLY the matching row as a YAML-formatted object with these fields:
domain, complexity, typical_concerns, compliance_requirements

**Do NOT return the entire CSV - only the matching row.**"

**Graceful degradation (if Task tool unavailable):**
- Load the CSV files directly
- Find the matching rows manually
- Extract required fields
- Keep in memory for intelligent classification

### 3. Begin Discovery Conversation

**Start with what you know:**

If the user has a product brief or project docs, acknowledge them and share your understanding. Then ask clarifying questions to deepen your understanding.

If this is a greenfield project with no docs, start with open-ended discovery:
- What problem does this solve?
- Who's it for?
- What excites you about building this?

**Listen for classification signals:**

As the user describes their product, match against:
- **Project type signals** (API, mobile, SaaS, etc.)
- **Domain signals** (healthcare, fintech, education, etc.)
- **Complexity indicators** (regulated industries, novel technology, etc.)

### 4. Confirm Classification

Once you have enough understanding, share your classification:

"I'm hearing this as:
- **Project Type:** {{detectedType}}
- **Domain:** {{detectedDomain}}
- **Complexity:** {{complexityLevel}}

Does this sound right to you?"

Let the user confirm or refine your classification.

### 5. Save Classification to Frontmatter

When user selects 'C', update frontmatter with classification:
```yaml
classification:
  projectType: {{projectType}}
  domain: {{domain}}
  complexity: {{complexityLevel}}
  projectContext: {{greenfield|brownfield}}
```

### N. Present MENU OPTIONS

Present the project classification for review, then display menu:

"Based on our conversation, I've discovered and classified your project.

**Here's the classification:**

**Project Type:** {{detectedType}}
**Domain:** {{detectedDomain}}
**Complexity:** {{complexityLevel}}
**Project Context:** {{greenfield|brownfield}}

**What would you like to do?**"

Display: "**Select:** [A] Advanced Elicitation [P] Party Mode [C] Continue to Product Vision (Step 2b of 13)"

#### Menu Handling Logic:
- IF A: Read fully and follow: {advancedElicitationTask} with the current classification, process the enhanced insights that come back, ask user if they accept the improvements, if yes update classification then redisplay menu, if no keep original classification then redisplay menu
- IF P: Read fully and follow: {partyModeWorkflow} with the current classification, process the collaborative insights, ask user if they accept the changes, if yes update classification then redisplay menu, if no keep original classification then redisplay menu
- IF C: Save classification to {outputFile} frontmatter, add this step name to the end of stepsCompleted array, then read fully and follow: {nextStepFile}
- IF Any other: help user respond, then redisplay menu

#### EXECUTION RULES:
- ALWAYS halt and wait for user input after presenting menu
- ONLY proceed to next step when user selects 'C'
- After other menu items execution, return to this menu

## CRITICAL STEP COMPLETION NOTE

ONLY WHEN [C continue option] is selected and [classification saved to frontmatter], will you then read fully and follow: `{nextStepFile}` to explore product vision.

---

## 🚨 SYSTEM SUCCESS/FAILURE METRICS

### ✅ SUCCESS:

- Document state checked and announced to user
- Classification data loaded and used intelligently
- Natural conversation to understand project type, domain, complexity
- Classification validated with user before saving
- Frontmatter updated with classification when C selected
- User's existing documents acknowledged and built upon

### ❌ SYSTEM FAILURE:

- Not reading documentCounts from frontmatter first
- Skipping classification data loading
- Generating executive summary or vision content (that's later steps!)
- Not validating classification with user
- Being prescriptive instead of having natural conversation
- Proceeding without user selecting 'C'

**Master Rule:** This is classification and understanding only. No content generation yet. Build on what the user already has. Have natural conversations, don't follow scripts.

- Agent Zero Integration: Specialized personas using custom skills (bmad_framework, bmad_reviewer, voice_mapper) and automation scripts (bmad_bridge.py, bt-init.py).

Product Vision:

Scaling from one crew to city-wide adoption via a V1-V4 roadmap including photo documentation and multi-site support.

- 80% crew adoption by day 3.

kvps.solutions:
# Problem
Integrating the BMAD (Breakthrough Method for Agile AI-Driven Development) framework with Agent Zero to build 'TaskFlow' (construction safety app), while maintaining project state and context across different AI conversation sessions.

# Solution
1. **Environment & Terminal Setup**:
   - Install BMAD in the project directory. 
   - **Terminal Fix**: Use a PTY wrapper to avoid 'RangeError': `stty cols 120 rows 40 2>/dev/null; script -qc 'COLUMNS=120 npx bmad-method install --directory . --tools none --yes' /dev/null`.

2. **State Tracking & Persistence**:
   - **Output Directory**: Use `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/` to store evolving documents (Product Brief/PRD).
   - **Frontmatter Metadata**: Use YAML frontmatter in markdown files to track progress (e.g., `stepsCompleted: [1, 2, 3]`) and metadata (date, author, input documents).
   - **A/P/C System**: Use the BMAD menu: `[A] Advanced Elicitation`, `[P] Party Mode`, or `[C] Continue` to save progress.

3. **Phase 1: Analysis Workflow Execution**:
   - **Persona**: Assume 'Business Analyst' (e.g., 'Mary'). Conduct interviews; do not generate autonomously.
   - **Step 1-6**: Progress from Initialization (Step 1) through Vision, User Discovery (Personas like 'Carlos' and 'Mike'), Success Metrics, and MVP Scope (Unit Board, Voice Commands) to Completion.
   - **Read First**: Always read the full step file (e.g., `step-01-init.md`) before acting.

4. **Session Handoff Protocol**:
   - Generate a structured 'Continuation Prompt' for new sessions including:
     - Absolute paths to project and BMAD directories.
     - Summary of TaskFlow project and key personas.
     - Current status (e.g., Phase 1 Analysis Complete).
     - Path to the next workflow (e.g., `_bmad/bmm/workflows/2-plan-workflows/create-prd/`).
     - Instructions for the new agent to act as a facilitator following the A/P/C system.

5. **Next Steps**: Transition to the `create-prd` workflow for user stories and technical specs.

---

From Step 1, I have loaded:
- **Product briefs:** 1
- **Research:** 0
- **Brainstorming:** 0
- **Project docs:** 0

This is a **greenfield project** — building TaskFlow from scratch.

---

## My Understanding from the Product Brief

I've reviewed the TaskFlow product brief thoroughly. Here's what I'm seeing:

TaskFlow is a **mobile-first web application** — a real-time digital task board for small construction remodeling crews. It's organized around **units** (physical workspaces) and **trades** (worker specializations), with the core interaction being checkbox taps and voice commands for task claiming. The key innovation is replacing the site superintendent as the coordination bottleneck with a self-service live board.

The target environment is pretty specific: dirty hands, job sites, smartphones, zero patience for complexity. The tech needs to be fast, simple, and real-time.

---

## Proposed Classification

Based on the brief, here's how I'd classify this:

| Dimension | Classification | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **Project Type** | `web_app` (Mobile-first PWA) | Browser-based, SPA with real-time updates, responsive/mobile-first design, voice interaction |
| **Domain** | `general` (Construction Operations) | No heavy regulatory requirements (no HIPAA, PCI, etc.). Standard software practices apply. Construction-specific but not in a regulated domain like building_automation or process_control |
| **Complexity** | `low` | Standard requirements, basic security (no auth in MVP), straightforward UX, real-time sync is the main technical challenge |
| **Project Context** | `greenfield` | Brand new product, no existing codebase or legacy systems |

---

**A couple of things I want to check with you:**

1. **Domain complexity** — I classified as `low` since the MVP has no authentication, no regulatory compliance, and no complex integrations. The real-time sync and voice commands add some technical complexity, but the domain itself is straightforward. Does that feel right, or do you see complexity I'm missing?

2. **Project type** — I'm leaning `web_app` (PWA) over `mobile_app` since the brief describes a browser-based experience rather than native iOS/Android. Sound right?

---

**Select:** **[A]** Advanced Elicitation · **[P]** Party Mode · **[C]** Continue to Product Vision (Step 2b of 13)"
    }
}

2. **Initialize Workflow State**: Check for the existence of the output file (e.g., `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/prd.md`). If it doesn't exist, proceed with a fresh initialization; if it exists but is incomplete, trigger the continuation protocol.
3. **Document Discovery**: Search the project structure (specifically `_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/`, `docs/`, and `_bmad-output/`) for relevant context files such as Product Briefs (`*brief*.md`), Research (`*research*.md`), and Project Context (`project-context.md`).
4. **Create Output Document**: Copy the PRD template from `_bmad/bmm/workflows/2-plan-workflows/create-prd/templates/prd-template.md` to the output path. Initialize the frontmatter with `stepsCompleted: [step-01-init]`, `inputDocuments: [list of found files]`, and document counts (e.g., `briefCount`, `researchCount`).
5. **Project Classification**: Load classification data from CSV files (`project-types.csv` and `domain-complexity.csv`). Analyze the discovered documents to propose a classification including `projectType` (e.g., web_app), `domain` (e.g., general), `complexity` (e.g., low), and `projectContext` (greenfield vs. brownfield).
6. **Collaborative Confirmation**: Present the discovery results and proposed classification to the user for confirmation before proceeding to the next step (Product Vision)."}

# Step 2b: Product Vision Discovery

**Progress: Step 2b of 13** - Next: Executive Summary

## STEP GOAL:

Discover what makes this product special and understand the product vision through collaborative conversation. No content generation — facilitation only.

## MANDATORY EXECUTION RULES (READ FIRST):

### Universal Rules:

- 🛑 NEVER generate content without user input
- 📖 CRITICAL: Read the complete step file before taking any action
- 🔄 CRITICAL: When loading next step with 'C', ensure the entire file is read
- ✅ ALWAYS treat this as collaborative discovery between PM peers
- 📋 YOU ARE A FACILITATOR, not a content generator
- ✅ YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT In your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`

### Role Reinforcement:

- ✅ You are a product-focused PM facilitator collaborating with an expert peer
- ✅ We engage in collaborative dialogue, not command-response
- ✅ You bring structured thinking and facilitation skills, while the user brings domain expertise and product vision

### Step-Specific Rules:

- 🎯 Focus on discovering vision and differentiator — no content generation yet
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to generate executive summary content (that's the next step)
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to append anything to the document in this step
- 💬 APPROACH: Natural conversation to understand what makes this product special
- 🎯 BUILD ON classification insights from step 2

## EXECUTION PROTOCOLS:

- 🎯 Show your analysis before taking any action
- ⚠️ Present A/P/C menu after vision discovery is complete
- 📖 Update frontmatter, adding this step to the end of the list of stepsCompleted
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to load next step until C is selected

## CONTEXT BOUNDARIES:

- Current document and frontmatter from steps 1 and 2 are available
- Project classification exists from step 2 (project type, domain, complexity, context)
- Input documents already loaded are in memory (product briefs, research, brainstorming, project docs)
- No executive summary content yet (that's step 2c)
- This step ONLY discovers — it does NOT write to the document

## YOUR TASK:

Discover the product vision and differentiator through natural conversation. Understand what makes this product unique and valuable before any content is written.

## VISION DISCOVERY SEQUENCE:

### 1. Acknowledge Classification Context

Reference the classification from step 2 and use it to frame the vision conversation:

"We've established this is a {{projectType}} in the {{domain}} domain with {{complexityLevel}} complexity. Now let's explore what makes this product special."

### 2. Explore What Makes It Special

Guide the conversation to uncover the product's unique value:

- **User delight:** "What would make users say 'this is exactly what I needed'?"
- **Differentiation moment:** "What's the moment where users realize this is different or better than alternatives?"
- **Core insight:** "What insight or approach makes this product possible or unique?"
- **Value proposition:** "If you had one sentence to explain why someone should use this over anything else, what would it be?"

### 3. Understand the Vision

Dig deeper into the product vision:

- **Problem framing:** "What's the real problem you're solving — not the surface symptom, but the deeper need?"
- **Future state:** "When this product is successful, what does the world look like for your users?"
- **Why now:** "Why is this the right time to build this?"

### 4. Validate Understanding

Reflect back what you've heard and confirm:

"Here's what I'm hearing about your vision and differentiator:

**Vision:** {{summarized_vision}}
**What Makes It Special:** {{summarized_differentiator}}
**Core Insight:** {{summarized_insight}}

Does this capture it? Anything I'm missing?"

Let the user confirm or refine your understanding.

### N. Present MENU OPTIONS

Present your understanding of the product vision for review, then display menu:

"Based on our conversation, I have a clear picture of your product vision and what makes it special. I'll use these insights to draft the Executive Summary in the next step.

**What would you like to do?**"

Display: "**Select:** [A] Advanced Elicitation [P] Party Mode [C] Continue to Executive Summary (Step 2c of 13)"

#### Menu Handling Logic:
- IF A: Read fully and follow: {advancedElicitationTask} with the current vision insights, process the enhanced insights that come back, ask user if they accept the improvements, if yes update understanding then redisplay menu, if no keep original understanding then redisplay menu

- ONLY proceed to next step when user selects 'C'

    "tool_name": "response",
    "tool_args": {
        "text": "# 🔭 Step 2b of 13: Product Vision Discovery

---

We've established TaskFlow is a **web app (mobile-first PWA)** in the **general/construction operations** domain with **low complexity**, built as a **greenfield** project. Now let's explore what makes this product special.

---

From the product brief, I'm already picking up some strong vision signals. Let me share what I'm hearing, and you tell me if I'm on the right track — or if there's something deeper I'm missing.

## What I'm Hearing

**The Core Insight:**
Construction coordination doesn't need a *management platform* — it needs a *communication replacement*. The real problem isn't project management, it's that Mike's brain is the system of record. TaskFlow isn't competing with Procore or Fieldwire; it's competing with *shouting across the site*.

**The Differentiator Moment:**
Carlos opens his phone at 7am, sees Unit 12 needs electrical rough-in, taps it, and walks straight there. No waiting for Mike. That's the "aha" — the first morning where nobody asks "where should I go?"

**The Deeper Need:**
It's not really about task tracking — it's about *autonomy for the crew*. Workers become self-directing instead of dependent on a single bottleneck. Mike gets to be a superintendent again instead of a human dispatcher.

---

## A Few Things I'd Love to Dig Into

1. **What's the one-sentence pitch?** If you're explaining TaskFlow to a site super at a bar, what do you say?

2. **Why now?** Every remodeling crew has had this problem forever. What makes this the right moment to solve it digitally? Is it smartphone ubiquity on job sites? Something else?

3. **What does success *feel* like?** Not the metrics — the vibe. When TaskFlow is working perfectly on a job site, what's different about the energy of that crew?

I want to make sure I'm capturing the soul of this product before we start writing anything."
    }
}

kvps.solutions:
# Problem
Implementing and executing the BMAD (Breakthrough Method for Agile AI-Driven Development) framework, specifically managing the transition from Phase 1 (Analysis) to Phase 2 (Planning/PRD) and executing the detailed PRD initialization workflow.

# Solution
1. **Environment & Terminal Setup**:
   - Install BMAD in the project directory (typically `_bmad/`).
   - **Terminal Fix**: Use a PTY wrapper to avoid 'RangeError': `stty cols 120 rows 40 2>/dev/null; script -qc 'COLUMNS=120 npx bmad-method install --directory . --tools none --yes' /dev/null`.

2. **Configuration & State Tracking**:
   - **Config**: Read `_bmad/bmm/config.yaml` to resolve `project_name`, `user_name`, and `planning_artifacts` paths.
   - **Persistence**: Use YAML frontmatter in documents (e.g., `stepsCompleted: [step-01-init]`, `inputDocuments: [...]`) to track progress.
   - **A/P/C System**: Use the BMAD menu: `[A] Advanced Elicitation`, `[P] Party Mode`, or `[C] Continue` to save/confirm/resume.

3. **Phase 2: PRD Initialization Workflow (Detailed)**:
   - **Step 1: Initialization & Discovery**: Locate workflow in `_bmad/bmm/workflows/2-plan-workflows/create-prd/`. Search for existing artifacts like Product Briefs (`*brief*.md`) or Research docs. Create the initial PRD from `../templates/prd-template.md`.
   - **Step 2: Project Classification**: Load data from `data/project-types.csv` and `data/domain-complexity.csv`. Analyze the brief to determine `projectType`, `domain`, `complexity`, and `projectContext` (greenfield vs. brownfield). Require user confirmation ('C') before updating frontmatter.
   - **Step 2b: Vision Discovery**: Facilitate a collaborative dialogue for 'Core Insight', 'Differentiator Moment', and 'Value Proposition' before generating content. Reference previous classification to frame the conversation.

4. **Session Handoff Protocol**:
   - Generate a 'Continuation Prompt' including absolute paths, project summary, current status (e.g., Phase 1 Complete), and the path to the next workflow.

Great — so here's my consolidated understanding of the TaskFlow vision:

---

**Vision:** Every remodeling crew has a self-organizing digital board that eliminates the superintendent as a coordination bottleneck — workers know where to go, what's ready, and what's blocked, without asking anyone.

**What Makes It Special:** It's not a project management tool — it's a *communication replacement*. Dead simple, dirty-hands friendly, zero training. Checkbox tap or voice command. That's it.

**Core Insight:** The problem isn't tracking tasks — it's that one person's brain is the entire coordination system. Replace that single point of failure with a shared, real-time board and the crew becomes self-directing.

---

# Step 2c: Executive Summary Generation

**Progress: Step 2c of 13** - Next: Success Criteria

## STEP GOAL:

Generate the Executive Summary content using insights from classification (step 2) and vision discovery (step 2b), then append it to the PRD document.

## MANDATORY EXECUTION RULES (READ FIRST):

### Universal Rules:

- 🛑 NEVER generate content without user input
- 📖 CRITICAL: Read the complete step file before taking any action
- 🔄 CRITICAL: When loading next step with 'C', ensure the entire file is read
- ✅ ALWAYS treat this as collaborative discovery between PM peers
- 📋 YOU ARE A FACILITATOR, not a content generator
- ✅ YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT In your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`

### Role Reinforcement:

- ✅ You are a product-focused PM facilitator collaborating with an expert peer
- ✅ We engage in collaborative dialogue, not command-response
- ✅ Content is drafted collaboratively — present for review before saving

### Step-Specific Rules:

- 🎯 Generate Executive Summary content based on discovered insights
- 💬 Present draft content for user review and refinement before appending
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to append content without user approval via 'C'
- 🎯 Content must be dense, precise, and zero-fluff (PRD quality standards)

## EXECUTION PROTOCOLS:

- 🎯 Show your analysis before taking any action
- ⚠️ Present A/P/C menu after generating executive summary content
- 💾 ONLY save when user chooses C (Continue)
- 📖 Update output file frontmatter, adding this step name to the end of the list of stepsCompleted
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to load next step until C is selected

## CONTEXT BOUNDARIES:

- Current document and frontmatter from steps 1, 2, and 2b are available
- Project classification exists from step 2 (project type, domain, complexity, context)
- Vision and differentiator insights exist from step 2b
- Input documents from step 1 are available (product briefs, research, brainstorming, project docs)
- This step generates and appends the first substantive content to the PRD

## YOUR TASK:

Draft the Executive Summary section using all discovered insights, present it for user review, and append it to the PRD document when approved.

## EXECUTIVE SUMMARY GENERATION SEQUENCE:

### 1. Synthesize Available Context

Review all available context before drafting:
- Classification from step 2: project type, domain, complexity, project context
- Vision and differentiator from step 2b: what makes this special, core insight
- Input documents: product briefs, research, brainstorming, project docs

### 2. Draft Executive Summary Content

Generate the Executive Summary section using the content structure below. Apply PRD quality standards:
- High information density — every sentence carries weight
- Zero fluff — no filler phrases or vague language
- Precise and actionable — clear, specific statements
- Dual-audience optimized — readable by humans, consumable by LLMs

### 3. Present Draft for Review

Present the drafted content to the user for review:

"Here's the Executive Summary I've drafted based on our discovery work. Please review and let me know if you'd like any changes:"

Show the full drafted content using the structure from the Content Structure section below.

Allow the user to:
- Request specific changes to any section
- Add missing information
- Refine the language or emphasis
- Approve as-is

### N. Present MENU OPTIONS

Present the executive summary content for user review, then display menu:

"Here's the Executive Summary for your PRD. Review the content above and let me know what you'd like to do."

Display: "**Select:** [A] Advanced Elicitation [P] Party Mode [C] Continue to Success Criteria (Step 3 of 13)"

#### Menu Handling Logic:
- IF A: Read fully and follow: {advancedElicitationTask} with the current executive summary content, process the enhanced content that comes back, ask user if they accept the improvements, if yes update content then redisplay menu, if no keep original content then redisplay menu
- IF P: Read fully and follow: {partyModeWorkflow} with the current executive summary content, process the collaborative improvements, ask user if they accept the changes, if yes update content then redisplay menu, if no keep original content then redisplay menu
- IF C: Append the final content to {outputFile}, update frontmatter by adding this step name to the end of the stepsCompleted array, then read fully and follow: {nextStepFile}
- IF Any other: help user respond, then redisplay menu

#### EXECUTION RULES:
- ALWAYS halt and wait for user input after presenting menu
- ONLY proceed to next step when user selects 'C'
- After other menu items execution, return to this menu

## APPEND TO DOCUMENT:

When user selects 'C', append the following content structure directly to the document:

```markdown
## Executive Summary

{vision_alignment_content}

### What Makes This Special

{product_differentiator_content}

## Project Classification

{project_classification_content}
```

Where:
- `{vision_alignment_content}` — Product vision, target users, and the problem being solved. Dense, precise summary drawn from step 2b vision discovery.
- `{product_differentiator_content}` — What makes this product unique, the core insight, and why users will choose it over alternatives. Drawn from step 2b differentiator discovery.
- `{project_classification_content}` — Project type, domain, complexity level, and project context (greenfield/brownfield). Drawn from step 2 classification.
